mapQuestions.question: Which TWO continents is Egypt on?
mapQuestions.answer: Mostly in Africa, but the Sinai Peninsula in the east is in Asia. The Suez Canal is the dividing line 🌍🌏

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Egypt?
mapQuestions.answer: Cairo, on the Nile. The wider Cairo area has over 22 million people, the biggest city in Africa.

mapQuestions.question: What is the longest river in the world, and what does it do for Egypt?
mapQuestions.answer: The Nile (6,650km). Egypt is mostly desert, and almost the entire population (about 110 million) lives in the thin green strip along the Nile.

mapQuestions.question: What separates Asia from Africa, and was a huge engineering project?
mapQuestions.answer: The Suez Canal, opened in 1869. It is 193km long and lets ships sail from the Mediterranean to the Red Sea without going all the way around Africa.

mapQuestions.question: What ocean and seas touch Egypt?
mapQuestions.answer: The Mediterranean Sea to the north, and the Red Sea to the east. The Red Sea coast is famous for amazing coral reefs.

foods.name: Koshari
foods.description: The national dish of Egypt. A pile of rice, lentils, macaroni and chickpeas, topped with a spiced tomato sauce, crispy fried onions and a vinegar-garlic sauce. Vegetarian, filling, deeply weird, completely delicious.

foods.name: Ful medames
foods.description: Slow-cooked fava beans mashed with olive oil, lemon, cumin and garlic. The Egyptian breakfast for thousands of years, scooped up with hot flatbread (aish baladi).

foods.name: Molokhia
foods.description: A bright green slimy soup made from jute leaves, garlic and coriander. Eaten with rice or bread. The texture is unusual at first. Ancient Egyptians ate it. It will probably outlast us all.

foods.name: Ta'meya
foods.description: Egyptian falafel. Made with fava beans (not chickpeas like the Lebanese version), fried crispy on the outside, fluffy and herby green inside. Often stuffed into pita with salad and tahini.

foods.name: Kebda
foods.description: Sautéed beef or lamb liver with garlic, chilli, cumin and bell peppers, eaten hot in a sandwich. Sold from carts everywhere, especially in Alexandria.

foods.name: Mahshi
foods.description: Vegetables (peppers, courgettes, aubergines, vine leaves) stuffed with spiced rice and herbs, then slow-cooked in a tomato broth. A favourite at family meals.

scavengerItems.label: A camel either being ridden or just standing around looking unbothered
A hieroglyph (or a souvenir with hieroglyphs on it)
A felucca (white-sailed wooden boat) on the Nile
A date palm tree heavy with bunches of dates
Men playing dominoes or backgammon outside an "ahwa" (coffee house)
A blue glass "eye of Horus" (a protective charm sold everywhere)
A street cat (Egyptians have considered cats sacred for over 4,000 years)
A pyramid in the distance from somewhere unexpected (Cairo neighbourhoods, motorway, etc.)
A horse-drawn cart hauling vegetables, sugar cane or rubbish through city streets
A papyrus painting in a shop (real ones are made from the river plant, ancient writing style)
A minaret on a mosque older than any building in your home country
The smell or sight of a "shisha" (water pipe) on a café terrace

geoMatches.description: The mega-capital. Old Islamic Cairo with thousand-year-old mosques, the National Museum stuffed with treasures, and the Pyramids visible from the suburbs.

On the edge of Cairo. The 4,500-year-old Pyramids and the Great Sphinx (a giant stone lion with a human head). The Pyramids of Giza are the only one of the original Seven Wonders of the Ancient World still standing.
The ancient city of Thebes, on the Nile. The Valley of the Kings (where pharaohs were buried, including Tutankhamun), the enormous Karnak temple, and balloon rides at sunrise.
The southernmost city, on a stretch of the Nile dotted with small green islands. The Nubian people of this region have their own language, music and food, and a slower pace.
The coastline along the Red Sea (Sharm el-Sheikh, Hurghada, Dahab). Calm clear water with some of the most colourful coral reefs in the world.
A long thin Mediterranean city in the north. Founded by Alexander the Great in 331 BC. Home of the ancient Lighthouse and Library, both long lost. Cooler, breezier and very different from Cairo.

senses.smell: Cumin and coriander, hot bread from a roadside oven, sweet apple shisha smoke from cafés, dry desert dust, jasmine in courtyards, the Nile at dawn…
senses.hear: Five calls to prayer a day from a thousand minarets, car horns as conversation, "yalla yalla" (let's go), domino tiles slapping on tables, the splash of feluccas on the Nile…
senses.taste: Crispy fava bean ta'meya, the salty tang of olive-oil ful, spicy koshari sauce, hot mint tea, fresh dates, salty Aleppo cheese, the buttery flake of feteer pastry…
senses.feel: Dry desert heat that punches you when you step outside, cool stone inside a tomb, the smooth wood of a felucca deck, hot Sahara wind, soft sand on bare feet…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How did people 4,500 years ago build the pyramids?
stories.body: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built around 2,560 BC, more than 4,500 years ago, for the pharaoh Khufu. It is 147 metres tall (originally), made from about 2.3 million stone blocks, each weighing 2.5 to 15 tonnes. For nearly 4,000 years it was the tallest human-made structure on earth, only beaten in 1311 by the cathedral spire of Lincoln in England. We still do not fully know how they did it. The current best theory: tens of thousands of (paid! not slaves!) workers, organised into teams with names like "Friends of Khufu", dragged the stones up huge mud-brick ramps. The whole thing took around 20 years. Inside are sealed corridors, false passages, and a single empty stone coffin.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Why do you think people 4,500 years ago wanted to build something this enormous, knowing they would not finish it in their lifetime?

stories.question: Why did the ancient Egyptians wrap their dead?
stories.body: Ancient Egyptians believed in an afterlife, but they thought you needed your body to enjoy it. So they perfected mummification: a 70-day process where the body was dried out with natural salt (called natron), the brain pulled out through the nose with a hook, the organs removed and stored in jars, and the whole body wrapped in hundreds of metres of linen bandages. They added charms and prayers between the layers. Wealthy pharaohs got gold masks. Pets got mummified too: archaeologists have found mummified cats, dogs, baboons, crocodiles and even ibis birds. The point was to keep the body identifiable to your soul (called the "ka") so it could find its way back home each night.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Egyptians thought you needed your body for the afterlife. What is one thing about you that you would most want to keep forever?

stories.question: Why is Egypt called "the gift of the Nile"?
stories.body: Egypt is around 95% desert. Without one river, no civilisation could ever have lived here. The Nile flows north for 6,650km, from deep in central Africa all the way to the Mediterranean. Every summer for thousands of years it used to flood, depositing rich black soil along its banks that was perfect for growing wheat, barley and flax. Ancient Egyptians called their country "Kemet", which meant "the Black Land", because of this dark fertile soil. Outside this thin green strip lay only "Deshret", the Red Land, the desert where nothing grows. Even today, 99% of Egyptians live within 12 miles of the Nile or its delta, on around 4% of the country's land.

stories.question: How was a forgotten boy-king's tomb found by accident?
stories.body: In 1922, a British archaeologist called Howard Carter had been searching the Valley of the Kings in Luxor for over 10 years, looking for the tomb of a pharaoh called Tutankhamun who had been almost forgotten by history. Tutankhamun (or "King Tut") had become pharaoh aged just 9, and died at 19. Most royal tombs had been broken into and robbed in ancient times, but Tut's tomb was small and hidden under a layer of stone chips from a later tomb being built on top, so the robbers missed it. On 4 November 1922, a worker found a stone step. By 26 November, Carter had broken through the sealed door and could see, in his words, "wonderful things". The tomb had 5,000 objects including a solid gold death mask. It is one of the greatest archaeological finds in history.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Carter spent 10 years looking before finding the tomb. Have you ever had to be patient about something for a really long time? What kept you going?

stories.question: How did one black stone teach us to read ancient Egyptian?
stories.body: For nearly 1,500 years, the hieroglyphs on Egyptian temples were a mystery. Nobody could read them. Then in 1799, during one of Napoleon's campaigns in Egypt, a French soldier found a slab of dark stone in the town of Rashid (Rosetta). The same message was carved on it three times: once in hieroglyphs (ancient Egyptian picture writing), once in demotic (a later Egyptian script), and once in ancient Greek, which scholars could already read. By comparing them, a young French linguist called Jean-François Champollion finally cracked the hieroglyphic code in 1822. Suddenly the writing on every tomb wall in Egypt could be read. The Rosetta Stone is now in the British Museum in London, and Egypt has been politely asking for it back for many years.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best Egyptian thing you've eaten?
How does it feel to stand next to something built 4,500 years ago?
What's one thing in modern Egypt that connects back to the ancient times?
Have you ridden a camel yet? Out of 10, how comfortable was it?
If you could go back to ancient Egypt for one day, what would you want to see?
Many of Egypt's greatest treasures (the Rosetta Stone, the Nefertiti bust, parts of mummies) are in museums in London, Berlin and Paris. Do you think they should go home?

wordSearch.hint: Egyptian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Rock dove
animals.description: Egypt domesticated pigeons thousands of years ago — they're everywhere.

animals.name: Dromedary camel
animals.description: Beside every pyramid, sphinx and tourist site. Riding them is optional.

animals.name: Egyptian street cat
animals.description: Sacred for thousands of years. Still in every alley, still acting like it owns the place.

animals.name: Working donkey
animals.description: Carrying sugarcane, vegetables, kids — still doing the work of small trucks in rural Egypt.

animals.name: Black kite
animals.description: Forked-tail bird of prey circling above Cairo and the Nile valley.

animals.name: Nile crocodile
animals.description: In Lake Nasser behind the Aswan dam (almost extinct downstream).

animals.name: Hieroglyph animal
animals.description: Spot any animal carved into a temple wall — cat, ibis, baboon, falcon, hippo.
